channel.title: Posts on Self Portrait
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Self Portrait

item.title: The Identity Panacea
item.description:
As a new father, I desperately looked for ways to put my young one to sleep regularly. After weeks of struggle, we came across a surprisingly simple solution - the sling wrap. The sling is basically a long piece of cloth typically 5 meters long and less than a meter wide, made of stretchy fabric. I wrap my son in it (like in the left picture above) and he goes to sleep in a few minutes usually. The dimensions of the sling wrap, now popular throughout the western world is so close to our very own saree, our traditional wear also famous for its multi-utility, when it struck me that the sling wrap is perhaps an Indian invention, now reapppearing in Europe. Such discoveries of India’s contributions usually makes me proud of my Indian identity. Just as I was about to cement that pride, a bolt of revelation struck me.

The baby sling is an invention much older than I thought. Perhaps older than the invention of the wheel itself. Timothy Taylor, an archeologist from the University of Bradford, in his book “The Artificial Ape”, speculates the invention of slings to about two million year ago! Mind you, humans as we know them–Homo Sapiens–have been around for about 200,000 years. The technology to cut and hunt have been dated to abut 50,000 years ago. This means that the sling is not a Homo Sapien invention, it is perhaps a proto-human invention. In fact the sling helped take gestation of babies out of the womb, enabling us to have bigger brains three times the size of our closest ape alive, the chimpanzee. To me, it is convincing because there must have been a genetic mutation that lead to premature birth of human babies making them incapable of locomotion soon as they were born. This mutation perhaps lead to the first artificial selection. Artificial selection is what has lead to the creation of thousands of breeds of dogs in the last couple of centuries, simply because human being chose them for a particular feature and ensured their further breeding. The genius of the sling made us mentally stronger allowing initial physical incapabilities. I want you to take this with a pinch of salt, just as I do. All this is just a possibility.

The possibility that humans invented millions of years ago, the kind of invention that makes me proud suggests that our inventivenes existed before identities began taking shape. Identity has been a central force in our history. Identification through religion, political borders, occupation and skin color takes prime time in our television debates today. What we have achieved, we ascribe to our upbringing, tied to our identity of some form. For most of us, identity is not even a possibility of genetic similarity. It is a superficial one, like being black and white.

This possibility put me to shame. Any human being with decent perception could have invented the sling. Anyone could have discovered the number zero, law of gravitation, the number system or even the planetary motion.

Identity is a central force in our history. Identification with a religion

item.title: Reading fast and slow
item.description:
As a studious kid in school, I have always looked out for ways to improve my learning efficiency. During the scramble for exam preparations, I came across a “speed reading” course. I got the course material delivered home in no time. It was a bunch of weird exercises like reading
inverted pages and reading without enunciating the words. Soon after, I was almost unable to read anything at all.

Often, we come across speed readers. The biography of Franklin D. Roosevelt claims that he read a book every single day. I have a friend who once read a 10,000 page fan fiction of Harry Potter in 8 days. How awesome it would be to scan through 300 pages in a couple of hours. With my speed I won’t finish all the books I want to read in my lifetime. No technique has ever helped me. Now, I have come to terms with my slow reading: very good terms.

“The tortoise inched steadily and outstripped the callous hare while it took a nap.” Some would skim through those words only to get to the next event in the story. Some would momentarily spawn a vivid visualization of it. They would have their own cinematic perspective—the tortoise looking back at the hare in slow motion. To slow readers like me, it is neither of these. A hundred sleeping hares from my life’s experiences emerge in front of me all at the same time. I have to choose between the literal and the figurative and stack the information in my mind’s shelves for information and for appreciation of language. I reread to reflect. I hunt my memory to check if this information is a branch of a thought tree that was thriving for a long time. The pause is sometimes more than just a moment.

The attempt to read faster took me farther from myself. I have realized that my way of reading doesn’t drift with the author. Instead, it is simply a process in the composition of my life’s experience. Hence a slow reader.

item.title: Faith
item.description: Faith in any form—religious faith or faith in ones own ability—is not above reason. Unwavering faith is a sharp tool, indeed. Faith leads to belief. Belief destroys doubt. This paves the way to tireless pursuits—the kind of pursuits that resulted in the man-made wonders of the world. In fact, some of the best minds invented it. Why? Both the simple minded and the intelligent feel and respond to pain. Faith is that thing that transcends this pain and fuses the person with the job in hand. Faith is an invented tool. When faith was taken to be above everything, genocides resulted. Faith needs to be in its place—its place as a tool. The intelligent mind created this tool after coming to terms with its own limits. It is consciousness that is superior to both! Now smile. :)
